title: Psalms 147
subtitle: How Good It Is to Sing Praises

sections.header: Verses 1–11 — “He Heals the Brokenhearted and Binds Up Their Wounds”

heb.paragraph: "He heals the brokenhearted (lishbūrē lēb) and binds up their wounds" (v.3). The God who counts the stars also bandages the broken (vv.3–4). "He determines the number of the stars and calls them each by name" (v.4). The scale: from naming galaxies to binding wounds. "Great is our Lord and mighty in power; his understanding has no limit" (v.5). He covers the sky with clouds, supplies rain, makes grass grow (vv.8–9). He does not delight in horse-strength or warrior-legs but in those who fear him and hope in his ḥesed (vv.10–11).

notes.note: "He heals the brokenhearted and binds up their wounds. He determines the number of the stars and calls them each by name." The juxtaposition: star-namer AND wound-binder. The same God who counts galaxies counts tears. This is the Psalter’s most stunning scale-shift.

"His pleasure is not in the strength of the horse, nor his delight in the legs of a warrior; the LORD delights in those who fear him, who put their hope in his unfailing love." God’s delight: not power but trust. Not strength but hope-in-ḥesed.

refs.note: God calls the stars by name; not one is missing—his power and understanding.
God knows every sparrow and numbers every hair—intimate and cosmic care.
Jesus invites the brokenhearted to come and find rest.

hist.context: Psalm 147 celebrates the God who rebuilds Jerusalem (post-exilic context) and heals the brokenhearted. The same God who counts and names the stars binds up wounds. This juxtaposition—cosmic power and intimate care—defines YHWH's character. The psalm may have been used at Jerusalem's rededication after the exile, celebrating both physical rebuilding and spiritual restoration.

sections.header: Verses 12–20 — “He Has Revealed His Word to Jacob”

heb.paragraph: "He sends his command (dəbārō) to the earth; his word runs swiftly" (v.15). God’s word controls nature: he spreads snow like wool, scatters frost like ashes, hurls hail like pebbles (vv.16–17). He sends his word and melts them; he stirs up breezes and the waters flow (v.18). "He has revealed his word to Jacob, his laws and decrees to Israel. He has done this for no other nation" (vv.19–20). The same word that governs weather governs Israel. Nature and Torah share one author. Hallelujah.

notes.note: "He sends his command to the earth. He spreads snow, scatters frost, hurls hail. He sends his word and melts them." The word-as-weather-control: snow, frost, hail, melting — all obeying the same command that governs Israel.
"He has revealed his word to Jacob. He has done this for no other nation." Israel’s unique privilege: the word that runs through nature was SPOKEN to them as Torah. Hallelujah.

refs.note: What nation has such righteous laws as Israel? Unique revelation.
The Jews were entrusted with the very words of God—a great advantage.
The law came through Moses; grace and truth came through Jesus Christ.

hist.context: The psalm's conclusion emphasizes Israel's unique privilege: 'He has revealed his word to Jacob, his laws and decrees to Israel. He has done this for no other nation.' This election theology acknowledges special revelation—the Torah given to Israel alone. Snow, frost, hail, ice, and warming winds all obey God's word; Israel alone has received his decrees. Praise flows from being the word-bearing people.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: The same God who counts galaxies counts tears…

ppl.name: Jacob
ppl.text: Jacob is a key figure in How Good It Is to Sing Praises.

For it is good to sing praises to our God; for it is pleasant, and a song of praise is fitting.NIVHe determines the number of the stars and calls them each by name.ESVHe determines the number of the stars; he gives to all of them their names.

rows.label: Word Runs Swift — 147:12–20
rows.text: "His WORD runs swiftly." Snow, frost, hail — all obeying. "He revealed his word to Jacob — no other nation." Hallelujah.

hebtext.note: "He heals the brokenhearted (lishbūrē lēb) and binds up their wounds" (v.3). The God who counts the stars also bandages the broken (vv.3–4). "He determines the number of the stars and calls them each by name" (v.4). The scale: from naming galaxies to binding wounds. "Great is our Lord and mighty in power; his understanding has no limit" (v.5). He covers the sky with clouds, supplies rain, makes grass grow (vv.8–9). He does not delight in horse-strength or warrior-legs but in those who fear him and hope in his ḥesed (vv.10–11).

"He sends his command (dəbārō) to the earth; his word runs swiftly" (v.15). God’s word controls nature: he spreads snow like wool, scatters frost like ashes, hurls hail like pebbles (vv.16–17). He sends his word and melts them; he stirs up breezes and the waters flow (v.18). "He has revealed his word to Jacob, his laws and decrees to Israel. He has done this for no other nation" (vv.19–20). The same word that governs weather governs Israel. Nature and Torah share one author. Hallelujah.

themes.note: Three categories at 9: Mercy (wound-binder), Sovereignty (star-namer, weather-commander), Worship (hallelujah). The word that governs nature governs Israel.
